assessor_1_raw:
## Independent Assessment 1: Readmission Prediction

The design includes a 7-day post-discharge utilization signal (ED presentations, outpatient follow-up, triage contacts, medication reconciliation within 7 days post-discharge) as a feature. If the model is deployed at discharge for intervention decisions, these events have not yet occurred. Including post-discharge events as features in a discharge-time prediction model constitutes temporal leakage — the model appears more accurate in evaluation than it can be at deployment.

**Verdict: critique_wins**
assessor_2_raw:
## Independent Assessment 2: Readmission Prediction

The 7-day post-discharge utilization feature includes events that occur after the prediction point. At discharge, the model doesn't know whether the patient will visit the ED in the next 7 days. Including this as a feature leaks post-discharge information into the evaluation. Both the sequence model and baseline have this feature, but that doesn't fix the leakage — it means both are contaminated and the comparison doesn't test the stated hypothesis.

**Verdict: critique_wins**
assessor_3_raw:
## Independent Assessment 3: Readmission Prediction

Major concern: the 7-day post-discharge utilization signal is post-discharge data in a model that should be making predictions at discharge. If clinical teams are supposed to use this model at discharge to target interventions, the post-discharge feature won't be available at that time. This is a deployment-time feature availability problem that will make the deployed model substantially worse than what the evaluation shows.

**Verdict: critique_wins**
adjudication_raw:
## Ensemble Synthesis

All three assessors: critique_wins. All independently identified the 7-day post-discharge utilization signal as temporal leakage for a discharge-time prediction model. issue_001 (target_leakage) detected unanimously.

**Verdict: critique_wins**
